title: When the Law Kills Your Electric Car Dealership
gist: The US Commerce Department denied Polestar authorization under a rule banning Chinese-made connected-vehicle technology, forcing the Geely-owned brand to stop US sales from the 2027 model year, while sister brand Volvo (also Geely-owned) was cleared in March, stranding US dealers who invested millions.

insights.claim: A US federal rule restricting sale of vehicles with Chinese-made connected-vehicle technology led Commerce to deny Polestar authorization, forcing it to stop US sales beginning with the 2027 model year.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: National-security tech rules can abruptly kill a foreign automaker's US market access; a regulatory risk template for other China-linked brands.
insights.quote: the US Commerce Department had denied an authorization that would have allowed the brand to continue selling cars in the US despite a federal rule restricting the sale of vehicles with Chinese-made connected-vehicle technology

insights.claim: The Commerce Department approved the connected-vehicle rule in January 2025 under the Biden Administration, banning Chinese- and Russian-made automotive cameras, microphones, and GPS hardware/software on national-security grounds.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: The rule is a bipartisan-durable structural barrier to Chinese automotive tech in the US, independent of the current administration.
insights.quote: It doesn't take much imagination to understand how a foreign adversary with access to this information could pose a serious risk to both our national security and the privacy of US citizens
insights.locator: The Commerce Department under the Biden Administration

insights.claim: Volvo, also majority-owned by Geely, received Commerce authorization in March 2026 to keep selling in the US after 'constructive discussions' on governance, technology and data security, while Polestar was denied.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Same parent, opposite outcomes suggests approval hinges on company-specific data-security posture and engagement, not just ownership; a template for compliance strategy.
insights.locator: Volvo, which is also majority-owned by Geely

insights.claim: The Polestar dealer explicitly blames Polestar's global management rather than the government for the denial, implying Volvo's clearance shows the outcome was avoidable.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Frames the loss as an execution/compliance failure, not pure geopolitics, a distinction relevant to whether other China-linked brands can adapt.
insights.quote: I think they really dropped the ball, and I blame them. I don't blame the government.
insights.locator: I am very frustrated in Polestar, globally

insights.claim: Owners of the US's 32 Polestar dealerships have invested many millions selling the cars and called the authorization denial a shock.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Regulatory bans impose stranded-capital losses on the dealer franchise layer, a downstream risk in the connected-vehicle policy regime.
insights.locator: he and the owners of the US's other 31 Polestar dealerships

insights.claim: Polestar framed the US exit as 'increasing its strategic focus on Europe' and noted 94 percent of its Q1 2026 sales occurred outside the US, a statistic the dealer calls misleading because the Polestar 4 only reached the US in December 2025 versus January 2024 in Europe.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: The low US sales share partly reflects delayed US product launches, not weak US demand; discount the company's spin on the exit.
insights.quote: 94 percent of Polestar's first quarter 2026 sales took place outside of the US

insights.claim: Polestar will sell existing Polestar 3 and Polestar 4 stock and maintain a US service network, and unlike bankrupt Fisker it still exists abroad and is legally obligated to support US vehicles.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Existing owners face less abandonment risk than a bankruptcy, but resale/residual values of stranded EVs are still at risk.
insights.locator: Unlike the electric vehicle company Fisker
